title: About Us | ExEF
body: Skip to main content About ExEF Executive function coaching grounded in neuroscience, built for adults, professionals, and students. We believe EF challenges deserve practical support. Explore Our Services Browse Free Resources Our Approach ExEF combines the executive function frameworks of Barkley, Brown, Dawson & Guare, and Ward for a grounded and effective coaching model. All resources and services reference named research frameworks with practicality at the core. Growth is formed over time, intentionally. Framework-First Coaching grounded in named models, systems, and support frameworks. All Ages From adults recently diagnosed with ADHD to professionals and students — we meet people where they are. Open Curriculum Our educational materials are publicly available. Paid work begins when 1:1 coaching, direct feedback, or hands-on planning support enters the picture. ExEF + Referral Network ExEF is a solo coaching practice. The educator and OT below are independent contractors and recommended referrals , not ExEF employees — you engage them directly under their own credentials. JR Jacob Rozansky Founder • Completing ADDCA ADHD coach training (ICF & PAAC accredited program) I started ExEF because I saw a gap between what the research says about executive function and the support most people actually get. Too often, EF challenges get reduced to "try harder" or "just use a planner." The brain works differently than that. Coaching grounded in the right frameworks meets people in context — at the point of performance, in real life. My ADDCA training is part of that standard: ADHD-specific coaching skills, clear scope boundaries, and practical support that fits the nervous system people actually have. jacob@exef.org Independent Referral Partners Different work belongs to different practitioners. The two below are listed as recommended references for needs outside ExEF coaching scope. CM Cole M. Independent referral • PhD, Occupational Therapy Sensory regulation and enviro

title: Guide: Barkley's Inhibition Model | ExEF
body: Skip to content A Guide to Barkley's Inhibition Model A practical interpretation of inhibition-first executive function science for educators and coaches. Three Stages of Inhibition 1. Stop Pause prepotent response 2. Interrupt Break in-progress action chain 3. Protect Shield goal from interference This sequence helps explain why students can "know what to do" yet still struggle in point-of-performance contexts. Coaching makes inhibition supports more visible and repeatable. Browse EF Resources Compare to Brown Model Inhibition cascades into the other executive functions. Further Sources: Barkley Citations 30 Essential Ideas 1B 30 Essential Ideas 5A 30 Essential Ideas 7B 30 Essential Ideas 9C 30 Essential Ideas 10E Open citations and source trails

title: Barkley vs Brown: Comparative Guide | ExEF
body: Skip to content Barkley vs Brown In ExEF's teaching use, Barkley is most useful for mechanism and Brown for symptom-pattern description, then interventions can draw from both views. Criterion Barkley Brown Core Question Why control fails in the moment How difficulties cluster in daily life Clinical Use Mechanism-level explanatory model Profile-level descriptive model Coaching Strength Designing inhibition supports Prioritizing intervention domains Common Limitation In Coaching Use Can feel less descriptive for lived profile detail Can be less explicit about inhibitory sequencing Different theoretical lenses refract the same construct differently. Run Brown Checklist Use This In Coaching Blog

title: Best-Fit Fitness Quiz | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Best-Fit Fitness Quiz Regular movement is one of the highest-leverage interventions for executive functioning. This 20-question quiz ranks 30 exercise formats against your real life, so you pick something you'll actually sustain — not the one that sounds most impressive. Educational tool, not a diagnosis. This is a self-reflection tool for coaching-style insight. It does not diagnose ADHD, executive function disorders, learning disabilities, or any mental-health or medical condition. For diagnostic evaluation, work with a licensed clinician. Why "best-fit" beats "best" Aerobic and resistance training both produce measurable gains in attention, working memory, and emotional regulation — but only when they're done consistently. The most common failure mode is choosing a format that's optimal in theory and abandoning it inside three weeks. We score your answers across four dimensions: energy match, logistical friction, social fit, and sensory tolerance . The top of the list is what's most likely to stick for you . Best used by: people restarting after a long gap, anyone who's bounced off three or more programs, and clients whose EF coaching plan needs a movement layer that won't compete with everything else. 0 of 31 answered How to answer: for each statement, pick the number that matches how true it feels for you . 0 Not true 2 Somewhat 4 Very true Please answer all 31 questions before scoring your fit list. Rank My Best-Fit Options Start over Your Results Your Best-Fit Movement Ranking Top three to test first Pick one . Schedule two sessions in the next seven days. The bottleneck is consistency, not selection. Full ranking across 43 options Scroll the full list when the top three don't fit your real life this week. How the ranking is built You answered across ten preference dimensions: solo vs. social, indoor vs. outdoor, intensity, structure, variety, equipment access, mind-body, joint health, and cost sensitivity. Each of the 43 options has a kn

title: The ExEF Beta Sprint | Executive Function Coaching
body: Skip to main content ExEF Core Offer The ExEF Beta Sprint A focused 4-session executive function coaching sprint for creative adults with ADHD who want practical help starting, planning, following through, and finishing the work that matters. Beta rate: $200 for 4 sessions — first 5 beta clients only. Book a 15-minute fit call ← Back to home What we do in the sprint 1. Friction Map Identify one primary EF friction point, one target situation, and one measurable goal. 2. System Build Build one practical system, one action plan, and one fallback plan. 3. Real-Life Test Review what actually happened and revise the system for real-world constraints. 4. Maintenance Plan Decide what to keep, what to continue, and whether ongoing support makes sense. Does this sound familiar? You know what to do, but cannot reliably start. You have too many ideas and no clear next step. Your plans disappear by mid-week. You wait until urgency or panic creates motion. You struggle with time blindness and follow-through. You want practical support, not another productivity lecture. Scope and safety ExEF provides executive function coaching and practical skills support. Coaching is not therapy, diagnosis, medical treatment, occupational therapy assessment, crisis support, or legal/academic accommodation documentation. If your needs fall outside coaching scope, ExEF may recommend an appropriate licensed professional. Boundary script and referral-out triggers Boundary script: “That’s outside the scope of coaching, and I don’t want to pretend I’m qualified to advise on it. I can help with the executive-function side, but I’d recommend bringing the clinical/legal/medical piece to the appropriate licensed professional.” Active self-harm or immediate crisis Severe depression/anxiety requiring clinical care Trauma processing, eating disorder behaviors, or substance-abuse crisis Medication advice, diagnosis requests, or legal/accommodation documentation needs Next step If this sounds useful, book a f

title: When EF Support Starts With Sleep and Movement | ExEF Blog
body: Skip to main content April 24, 2026 When EF Support Starts With Sleep and Movement People often try to fix executive-function problems with increasingly complex planners, apps, and routines. Sometimes that works. Often it does not—because the real constraint is physiological load, not strategy quality. Two inputs usually change the game fastest: sleep regularity and movement consistency. Not perfect sleep. Not elite training. Just enough baseline stability that your brain can access planning, inhibition, and working memory when you ask it to. A practical order of operations Set one wake-time anchor. This is usually more impactful than chasing a perfect bedtime. Pick one repeatable movement format. “Good enough and repeatable” beats “ideal and abandoned.” Reduce friction before adding ambition. Place shoes, prep clothes, and decide the first 10 minutes the night before. Once those supports are stable, planning tools suddenly feel less like punishment and more like leverage. Take the Fitness Quiz Take the Sleep Quiz ↑

title: Blog | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Blog April 24, 2026 When EF Support Starts With Sleep and Movement Before adding complex planning systems, stabilize physiology. This post lays out a practical sequence for improving executive functioning through sleep anchors and repeatable movement. Read post → ↑

title: Brown's 6 Clusters Pre-Diagnostic Tool | ExEF
body: Skip to content Brown's 6 Clusters: Pre-Diagnostic Checklist This is an educational screener for pattern recognition, not a diagnosis. Brown's six-cluster map for conversation structuring and intervention targeting. Rate Struggle Level Select one statement per cluster. 1. Activation (starting/organizing) Minimal Mild Moderate High 2. Focus Minimal Mild Moderate High 3. Effort Minimal Mild Moderate High 4. Emotion Minimal Mild Moderate High 5. Memory Minimal Mild Moderate High 6. Action Monitoring Minimal Mild Moderate High Generate Pattern Summary Browse Resources Explore Coaching After You Score Use your top two clusters to choose one environmental intervention and one skill-building intervention for the next seven days. Further Sources: Brown Citations CHADD article: six aspects of executive function Brown EF model summary Brown EF/A scales overview handout Open citations and source trails

title: Creative Coaching Methods | ExEF
body: Skip to main content ← Back to EF Services Creative Engagement Methods ExEF coaching integrates improv, chess, and cubing as structured executive function tools — not just games, but deliberate methods for building focus, flexibility, and self-regulation through activities students genuinely engage with. The Creative-to-Structured Coaching Approach Each method below targets specific EF skills through structured play. The progression moves from activation and engagement to deliberate practice and real-world transfer. Improv — "Yes, And..." Framework Targets: Cognitive flexibility, verbal working memory, social reciprocity, activation How it works: Improvisation exercises build the "say yes to the moment" habit — a direct counter to rigid thinking and shutdown responses. Students practice generating ideas under low-stakes time pressure. Best for: Students with rigid thinking patterns, social anxiety around collaboration, or difficulty starting tasks without a perfect plan. Chess — "Checkmate" Protocol Targets: Planning, inhibition, consequence awareness, working memory How it works: Chess positions are used as structured decision labs. Students practice pausing before acting, weighing alternatives, and tracking multiple variables — the same skills required for homework planning and impulse management. Best for: Students who are impulsive, rush through work, or struggle to think more than one step ahead. Cubing — "Algorithm" Approach Targets: Procedural memory, frustration tolerance, task decomposition, sustained attention How it works: Learning to solve a Rubik's cube teaches step-by-step decomposition of a complex task. Students build confidence by mastering algorithms and develop tolerance for the discomfort of not-yet-knowing. Best for: Students who give up easily, struggle with multi-step instructions, or avoid tasks that feel overwhelming. What Creative Coaching Looks Like in Practice During Sessions Structured games alternate with reflection and real-world plann

title: Conative Action Profile | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Conative Action Profile Discover how you naturally initiate action. This assessment measures your instinctive operating mode across four dimensions — not personality or intelligence, but how you do when free to be yourself. Back to Assessment Hub Open Cross-Signal Profile Educational tool, not a diagnosis. This is a self-reflection tool for coaching-style insight. It does not diagnose ADHD, executive function disorders, learning disabilities, or any mental-health or medical condition. For diagnostic evaluation, work with a licensed clinician. What This Measures The Conative Action Profile maps four modes of taking action: how you gather information, build systems, embrace change, and work with tangible materials. Everyone uses all four — the profile shows where you lean hardest when striving. Fact Finder — How you gather and use information. Follow Thru — How you organize and create systems. Quick Start — How you handle risk and change. Implementor — How you work with tangible materials. How To Take This You will face 36 scenarios across six life domains. For each, pick the action you are MOST likely to do. Then, pick the action you are LEAST likely to do. Trust your gut. Answer how you naturally act when free to be yourself, not how you think you should act. Takes approximately 10–15 minutes . Start Assessment This tool uses a forced-choice methodology to measure conative behavior. It is an independent, non-diagnostic tool and is not affiliated with any trademarked assessment product. Page 1 of 6 — 0 of 36 answered Back Next Page Your Action Profile This is your natural conative blueprint. It shows how you initiate action and solve problems when striving. There are no bad scores. Copy Summary Export Text Retake Assessment Recent Conative snapshots Next Step In The Assessment Sequence Pair your Conative Action Profile with ESQ-R or Task Start Friction data to see how your natural action mode interacts with your executive functioning patterns. title: Free Executive Functioning Quiz: EF Profile Story | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Free EF Profile Story Quiz Rate each statement from 1 to 5 and ExEF will generate a narrative executive functioning profile showing where friction shows up, what is already working, and a 7-day experiment to test this week. Back to Assessment Hub Educational tool, not a diagnosis. This is a self-reflection tool for coaching-style insight. It does not diagnose ADHD, executive function disorders, learning disabilities, or any mental-health or medical condition. For diagnostic evaluation, work with a licensed clinician. Each assessment is one piece of the larger profile. Scale 1 = Strongly disagree and 5 = Strongly agree . These statements describe friction patterns, so higher scores indicate more daily drag in that area — the opposite of capacity-style scales like the ESQ-R . Don't compare raw numbers across the two tools; compare the cluster shape instead. 0 of 16 answered Generate My Profile Story Reset Your Result Profile Title Your Profile Narrative Where You Get Stuck What Already Works Your 7-Day Experiment Dimension Friction Scores Copy Summary Export Text Generate a profile to enable export actions. title: Executive Functioning Environment Quiz | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Executive Functioning Environment Quiz Most executive-function advice focuses on habits and motivation. This quiz asks a more useful question first: is your environment quietly helping your brain, or quietly draining it? Back to Assessment Hub Start The Quiz Educational tool, not a diagnosis. This is a self-reflection tool for coaching-style insight. It does not diagnose ADHD, executive function disorders, learning disabilities, or any mental-health or medical condition. For diagnostic evaluation, work with a licensed clinician. What This Measures Your environment across sensory load, visibility, task friction, pacing, digital systems, social support, recovery, and flexibility. Who It Helps People with ADHD, executive dysfunction, time blindness, burnout, or chronic “why does this feel harder than it should?” patterns. How To Answer Rate each statement from 0 to 4 based on your usual environment, not your ideal setup and not your best day. What You Get A total support score, per-domain friction map, fast interpretation, top quick wins, and a short action plan you can use right away. Important framing This tool supports self-awareness and planning. Diagnostic decisions belong with licensed medical or mental health professionals. Its job here is to show where your environment is acting like an external scaffold for executive functioning and where it is adding invisible friction. Environment acts as invisible scaffolding — or invisible friction. 0 of 40 answered Scale 0 Never true 1 Rarely true 2 Sometimes true 3 Usually true 4 Consistently true Please answer all 40 questions before generating your environment report. Generate My Environment Report Reset Your Result Your environment report Overall support score 0 / 160 Fast Read Domain Scorecard Your lowest one or two domains matter more than your total. One weak environmental layer can destabilize everything else. Highest-Friction Zones Strongest Support Zones Top Quick Wins These are the fastest

title: Free Executive Functioning Test (ESQ-R) | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Free ESQ-R Interactive Intake ExEF's live ESQ-R tool gives you a fast, structured read on five executive-function areas, then points you to the next useful tool or support path. Back to Assessment Hub Open Cross-Signal Profile Educational tool, not a diagnosis. This is a self-reflection tool for coaching-style insight. It does not diagnose ADHD, executive function disorders, learning disabilities, or any mental-health or medical condition. For diagnostic evaluation, work with a licensed clinician. What This Measures Planning and time management, task initiation and follow-through, attention and working memory, organization and self-monitoring, and emotional regulation and flexibility. Best First Use Use ESQ-R first if you want a broad, non-diagnostic intake pattern before choosing a more specific ExEF tool or a paid service. How To Answer Rate each statement from 1 to 5 based on how you usually function, not on your best day. Higher scores mean stronger current capacity in that area — the opposite of friction-style scales like the EF Profile Story , so don't compare raw numbers across tools. What You Get A clearer result narrative, two current strengths, two leverage points, and a recommended path into the other ExEF tools. Important framing This tool is for self-awareness, planning, and coaching-style reflection. It is not a diagnosis and should not be treated as one. 0 of 30 answered Please answer all 30 questions before generating your profile. title: Executive Functioning IEP Goal Bank | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Executive Functioning IEP Goal Bank A practical drafting desk for EF goals: pick the skill, match the grade band, choose the data method, then customize the goal from the student's baseline. Start Here Browse Goals Pick Data Method IEP Use Boundary These goals are informational examples. IDEA requires IEP goals to be individualized from the student's PLAAFP, evaluation data, baseline performance, disability-related needs, and IEP team decisions. Do not copy a goal into a legally binding IEP without adapting the condition, behavior, criterion, and measurement method to the student's documented baseline. Quick Start Build A Better EF Goal In Four Moves Most weak EF goals fail because they skip the baseline or use vague measurement. Use this sequence before browsing examples. 1. Name The Skill Choose the EF demand that is actually blocking access: initiation, planning, organization, time, memory, flexibility, monitoring, regulation, inhibition, attention, or persistence. 2. Add Baseline Anchor the goal in PLAAFP data: current prompt level, latency, missing-work rate, rubric score, completion percentage, or observed frequency. 3. Pick A Scaffold Write the support into the condition: checklist, visual timer, planner, graphic organizer, self-rating rubric, or digital calendar. 4. Choose Data Decide how the team will measure it before the goal is finalized. If no one can collect the data, the goal is not ready. Goal Formula Given [condition/scaffold], the student will [observable EF behavior], to [criterion], as measured by [specific data source] over [review period]. Copy Goal Formula Problem Finder Start With The School Problem If you do not know the EF domain yet, start with the observable classroom problem. These buttons jump straight to the most likely goal area. Won't Start Work Look at latency, prompt dependence, and first-step behavior. Open Task Initiation Lost Or Missing Assignments Look at systems for materials, portals, folders, and turn-in

title: ExEF Cross-Signal Profile | Free EF Quizzes & Tests
body: Skip to main content ExEF Cross-Signal Profile Complete multiple ExEF diagnostics, then generate one unified profile model. ExEF synthesizes recurring patterns across planning, activation, regulation, and environment instead of treating each quiz as a separate island. Back to Assessment Hub Educational tool, not a diagnosis. This is a self-reflection tool for coaching-style insight. It does not diagnose ADHD, executive function disorders, learning disabilities, or any mental-health or medical condition. For diagnostic evaluation, work with a licensed clinician. How The ExEF Model Works ExEF uses a named synthesis layer, not just a stitched-together dashboard. At least two diagnostics are required before the model becomes meaningful. Four Cross-Signal Domains Planning, Activation, Regulation, and Environment. Output One profile frame, one leverage point, and one 7-day action plan. Diagnostic progress 0 of 5 diagnostics complete Complete at least two diagnostics to unlock the Cross-Signal Profile. Finish all five for the strongest pattern read. Take Free ESQ-R Take EF Profile Story Run Task Start Friction Open Time Calibrator Take Action Profile Sample Output Full Sample Cross-Signal Profile A concrete example of what the combined model is trying to produce. Profile Frame Pressure-Sensitive Activation Pattern This sample client understands priorities but stalls when tasks carry visibility, ambiguity, or emotional risk. The startup cost is the main problem, not intelligence or commitment. Best leverage point: lower task threat and create a tiny visible first action before asking for full focus. Signal Strengths Activation: high signal across task start friction and EF story patterning Regulation: moderate signal when evaluation or urgency spikes Planning: moderate signal tied to optimistic timing and overcommitment Environment: light signal; clutter and interruptions make the startup cost worse Top Priorities Replace giant task lists with one first move per priority Us

title: Build a Free AI Email Organizer | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Build a Free AI Email Organizer Use the Gemini API and Google Apps Script to sort, summarize, and manage your inbox automatically — no coding required. What You'll Build An AI bot that lives inside your Google account Sorts emails into categories you define Sends you a daily digest Can draft replies and auto-archive low-priority mail Runs on autopilot every day The Gemini API costs pennies per day for this kind of usage — typically under $1/month. Paid email organizer services that do the same thing charge $20/month or more. Before You Start A Gmail account (personal or Google Workspace) About 15 minutes Willingness to copy and paste Access to any AI chatbot (Claude, Gemini, ChatGPT, etc.) Step-by-Step Walkthrough Step 1: Open Google Apps Script Go to script.google.com and sign in with the Gmail account you want to organize. Click New project and name it Email Organizer . Make sure you're signed into the right Google account if you have multiple. Step 2: Get a Gemini API Key Go to aistudio.google.com/apikey . Click Create API key and copy it. In Apps Script, go to Project Settings > Script Properties > add a property named GEMINI_API_KEY (exact spelling, no spaces, case-sensitive) with your key as the value. Keep your API key out of chat windows. Never paste the key itself into the AI that writes your script — only tell it the property name. If you ever paste the key into any chat, document, or screenshot, treat it as leaked and rotate it at aistudio.google.com/apikey . Step 3: Decide Free Tier vs. Paid (and enable billing if you need it) Gemini has a generous free tier. Most people never pay a cent. But if you have a 10k+ backlog or want very frequent runs, you'll hit the free-tier rate limits and the script will start failing with 429 errors. Here's how to tell which camp you're in, and what to do if you need the paid tier. You're almost certainly fine on the free tier if: Your backlog is under ~5,000 emails. The bot runs once an hour or less

title: ExEF | EF & ADHD Coaching
body: Skip to main content ExEF: Executive Function support for adults, students, and professionals who know what to do but can’t seem to start. The ExEF Beta Sprint is open now: 4 sessions for $200 for the first 5 beta clients. Book a fit call to see if coaching is the right support for your next 30 days. Book a 15-minute fit call See Beta Sprint details Led by Jacob Rozansky , completing ADDCA ADHD coach training (ICF & PAAC accredited program), with a short list of independent referral partners for needs outside coaching scope — San Diego & online. Or try the free assessments first. Why this works EF is an execution problem, not a knowledge problem. If you’ve already read the books and tried the planners, you don’t need more information. You need a system you can actually run on a Tuesday at 4 p.m. Targets the point of performance Coaching plugs in where the work actually happens — the desk, the calendar, the inbox — not in the abstract. “ADHD is a disorder of doing what you know.” — Russell A. Barkley, PhD Build one system, then test it We design a small, specific scaffold, run it through real life, and revise. No grand productivity overhauls. “Implementing what you already know, consistently, over time.” — J. Russell Ramsay, PhD See the finish, plan the start Most stuck-ness is invisible until we put language and structure on it. Then it becomes workable. “Picture yourself doing a task and work backward to now.” — Sarah Ward, MS, CCC-SLP Stay In The Loop Get tools that make executive function feel more workable. Short emails. Useful releases. More clarity, less shame. Name Email I want ExEF email updates and understand I can unsubscribe any time. Join the List No thanks Coaching is the core offer Free tools show you the pattern. Coaching builds the system. Most people who land here have already tried the productivity advice, the planners, and the apps. What changes things is structured 1:1 work that fits how your brain actually moves — intake, environmental redesign,

title: Meet the Team | ExEF

The educators and clinicians below are independent contractors and recommended referrals — not ExEF employees. Why it matters: keeping clinicians off ExEF’s payroll keeps licensing, liability, and scope-of-practice lines clean. You hire each professional directly under their own credentials and terms. Use it when you want the larger open ecosystem, not just ExEF's primary teaching set. Classroom EF Support Resource Practical classroom-facing supports for identifying and reducing executive load. title: Personal Operating Manual Builder | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Personal Operating Manual Builder Turn a static personal operating manual template into a living, interactive guide you can actually complete, revisit, and download. Built for distributed teams, coaching clients, and anyone who wants a cleaner “how to work with me” document than a static PDF can provide. Start Your Manual Jump To Preview What you get Prompted sections based on the original template Checkbox shortcuts plus open-ended reflection fields Live preview with downloadable text and HTML exports Draft saved in this browser while you work What This Is A personal operating manual is a practical “how to work with me” guide. It helps people understand your schedule, communication style, strengths, and limits before confusion starts. How To Use It You do not need to answer every question. Start with the checkboxes, then only write out the parts that would genuinely help another person work with you better. Why It Helps Clear expectations reduce friction. A good manual makes onboarding, feedback, handoffs, and everyday collaboration easier for everyone around you. What To Download When you are done, export a plain-text version for docs or chat, or a polished HTML version for a wiki, onboarding page, or team handbook. Fastest Good Version Fill these first if you want a useful manual fast Your schedule and when people should not expect a reply Your best contact method for normal questions versus urgent issues How you prefer feedback and written follow-up What stress looks like for you and what actually helps The topics or work people can reliably come to you for Source note This builder follows a classic personal operating manual structure and adapts it into a cleaner, more flexible format for modern teams, clients, and collaborators. Cover Sheet Your Manual Basics Your name Role Team or org Pronouns Primary time zone Location or work context Photo for this session and HTML export Photo caption Remove photo Progress 0 of 36 prompts completed Star

title: Coaching vs Therapy Scope of Practice | ExEF
body: Skip to content Coaching vs Therapy: Scope of Practice Operational boundaries, non-diagnostic language, referral triggers, and ethical limits across ExEF's coaching, educational specialist, and OT support lanes. Area EF Coaching Educational Specialist Occupational Therapy Therapy / Clinical Care Primary Aim Skill building + implementation systems Academic access, IEP/504 translation, learning systems Sensory regulation, motor planning, daily living skills Mental health diagnosis/treatment Primary Method Planning, routines, accountability design Document review, school advocacy, study/homework systems Sensory integration, ADL training, embodied regulation Clinical interventions, psychotherapy protocols Assessment Role Educational and coaching-oriented interpretation Educational testing review and accommodations mapping OT evaluation: sensory, motor, ADL, visual-motor Clinical evaluation and diagnosis School Support Role Preparation, parent education, accommodation translation IEP/504 attendance, advocacy, academic remediation plans Sensory-friendly environmental consultation, AT recommendations Legal representation or licensed advocacy where required Referral Trigger Persistent safety concerns or clinical symptom escalation Sensory/motor barriers driving the breakdown → OT; clinical symptoms → therapy Diagnosed mental-health concerns or learning-disability evaluation needs Appropriate intake into treatment pathway Critical Practice Rule When risk of harm, severe mood instability, active trauma symptoms, or other urgent mental-health concerns are present, pause coaching scope and refer to licensed clinical providers or emergency resources. ExEF Does Not Represent Coaching As Medical or psychological diagnosis Psychotherapy or mental-health treatment Legal advice or attorney representation Guaranteed educational, financial, or clinical outcomes ExEF Coaching May Include Planning systems and accountability design Parent preparation for school meetings Accommodation tran

title: Sleep & Executive Function Quiz | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Sleep & Executive Function Quiz If your planning, focus, and emotional regulation feel harder than they should, sleep quality may be a hidden multiplier. This 16-question check helps you test that hypothesis in about three minutes. Educational tool, not a diagnosis. This is a self-reflection tool for coaching-style insight. It does not diagnose ADHD, executive function disorders, learning disabilities, or any mental-health or medical condition. For diagnostic evaluation, work with a licensed clinician. Why sleep is an EF lever The prefrontal cortex — the seat of working memory, inhibition, and planning — is the first system to degrade under sleep debt. Even mild chronic shortfall (45–60 minutes a night for two weeks) measurably reduces sustained attention, emotional regulation, and decision quality. People often work on time-management strategies for months before realizing the underlying constraint is biological, not behavioral. Use this quiz when: EF strategies you know should work keep failing, you wake unrefreshed, or you suspect a hidden cost from late nights, screens, caffeine timing, or inconsistent schedules. 0 of 16 answered Please answer all 16 prompts before generating your sleep readout. Generate Sleep Readout Reset Your sleep result Domain breakdown What to do this week Pair this with Sleep is one signal. To see whether it's the binding constraint on your EF, cross-reference it with one or two of these: Best-Fit Fitness Quiz — movement and sleep are the two biggest non-medication levers for prefrontal function. Time Blindness Calibrator — sleep debt amplifies time-perception errors. Cross-Signal EF Profile — combine sleep, time, friction, and ESQ-R results into a single picture. If results suggest a clinical sleep issue (loud snoring, witnessed apnea, severe insomnia), please talk to a physician — this quiz is educational, not diagnostic. ↑

title: Task Start Friction Diagnostic | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Task Start Friction Diagnostic Pick a real situation, rate the friction layers that are getting in the way, and ExEF will build a tailored start script you can use immediately. Educational tool, not a diagnosis. This is a self-reflection tool for coaching-style insight. It does not diagnose ADHD, executive function disorders, learning disabilities, or any mental-health or medical condition. For diagnostic evaluation, work with a licensed clinician. Best Use Use this when the challenge is getting started. The diagnostic works best when you apply it to one real task instead of a vague category. Friction leaves evidence on the things you touch while stuck. Choose A Situation Scenario A report due tomorrow Studying for a test you have been putting off Dealing with a missing assignment An important email you need to send Starting a proposal, project plan, or presentation Making a call you do not want to make Resetting a messy room or workspace Paying a bill or handling a money admin task Booking an appointment you have delayed A form or application you have been avoiding Choose a real scenario and ExEF will tailor the friction prompts and first-step protocol. Rate The Friction Layers Build Start Script Reset Copy Protocol Export Text No protocol copied yet. Your Start Protocol Rate each friction layer and ExEF will build a tailored start script for this situation. Next Step In The Assessment Sequence Pair this start-friction result with ESQ-R, EF Profile Story, or Time Blindness data when you want ExEF to name the bigger pattern instead of a single moment. Open Time Blindness Calibrator Open Cross-Signal Model

title: The Special Educator's Guide to Becoming an EF Coach | ExEF
body: Skip to content The Special Educator's Guide to Becoming an EF Coach This is ExEF's educator route: start here if you teach now, want stronger executive-function implementation tools, or are evaluating a move into coaching. Best order: use the free launchpad and ROI planning first, inspect standards second, and only then compare reviewed services or enrollment pricing. Educator Route Sequence Use this page in three moves This page helps educators test the transition path in the right order before they spend money. Step 1: use ROI and launchpad tools to test whether a coaching shift is actually viable Step 2: inspect certification standards and public artifacts if the role change still fits Step 3: compare reviewed services only after the standards and scope are clear Start Free Launchpad Review Standards Compare Reviewed Services Transition Realities From grading compliance to building self-regulation From district constraints to scalable private practice From fixed salary to client-capacity planning Start Free Launchpad Check Skills Gap Coaching ROI Calculator Estimate the weekly client load needed to model a target income at common rate points. title: Time Blindness Calibrator | ExEF
body: Skip to main content Time Blindness Calibrator Compare your first-draft time estimates against realistic ExEF planning benchmarks. After a few entries, the tool calculates your timing drift, confidence band, and a correction factor you can reuse this week. Educational tool, not a diagnosis. This is a self-reflection tool for coaching-style insight. It does not diagnose ADHD, executive function disorders, learning disabilities, or any mental-health or medical condition. For diagnostic evaluation, work with a licensed clinician. Best Use Use this after ESQ-R or alongside it if planning and time realism are major friction points. Add at least three entries for a usable pattern. Add A Benchmark Check Context Choose a context... Task Choose a context first... Your Estimate (minutes) Task Complexity Easier than usual About typical Harder than usual How confident are you in that estimate? Low confidence Moderate confidence High confidence ExEF Benchmark Note Choose a context and task to see ExEF's typical time benchmark.
